text.title: On-Call Rotation Bootstrap Failure Modes
text.summary: 5 antipatterns observed in real deployments. Each pairs symptom + root cause + concrete fix.

antipattern.description: Single-engineer rotation in a team of 8+; burnout accumulates silently, surfaces as resignation 6-12 months in.
antipattern.reason: Team grew but rotation design was not revisited; the founding engineer remains de-facto on-call.
antipattern.note: Fix: Add a secondary tier. Re-cut schedule. Apply comp policy to both tiers (secondary off-hours pages count).

antipattern.description: No charter, no paging tool, no schedule; on-call is whoever is in Slack and notices the alert.
antipattern.reason: Team postponed paging-tool setup; ad-hoc behaviour calcified.
antipattern.note: Fix: Stand up the paging tool, commit schedule config to version control, route alerts through the tool. Publish the charter as prerequisite to any further alert tuning.

antipattern.description: Engineers told 'we appreciate your off-hours work' with no actual time-off-in-lieu or cash compensation accruing.
antipattern.reason: Comp policy not formalised in HR handbook; no automatic accrual engine.
antipattern.note: Fix: Draft and ship the comp policy. Backfill 90 days. Make comp accrual automatic from paging-tool events.

antipattern.description: 10+ pages per shift, most non-actionable; alert-blindness develops.
antipattern.reason: Alerts proliferate without runbooks; no noise budget.
antipattern.note: Fix: Halt new rotation participation. Run an alert-tuning week. Re-open rotation when median pages/shift < 3.

antipattern.description: New engineer's first shift is solo primary; first incident is sev-1; MTTR spikes.
antipattern.reason: Shadow-period rule not enforced in the scheduling tool.
antipattern.note: Fix: Block solo primary assignment without shadow record. Enforce 2 shadow + 2 supervised solo before solo.
